transcripts.title: 10 Common IELTS Writing Task 2 Topics and Band 9 Samples

segments.text: Many IELTS candidates lose marks in writing task
2, not because they can't write in English,
but because they are not prepared for the
topics that come up in the writing test.
Here's the truth.
Certain themes appear again and again in the
test.
If you prepare vocabulary, grammar structures, and key
ideas for these topics in advance, you will
be in a much stronger position when you
sit down to write.
We've helped thousands of students prepare this way
through our IELTS packages.
They discover how strategic preparation can completely change
their results.
In this video, we'll go through 10 common
IELTS writing task 2 topics, and you'll get
high-scoring vocabulary and grammar for each one.
By the end, you'll know which topics to
prepare for IELTS writing task 2.
Let's get started.
Topic number one is health.
This topic comes up regularly in IELTS writing
task 2, so it's essential that you prepare
for it.
Questions often focus on the causes of poor
health in modern society.
One common question is whether individuals or governments
are responsible for public well-being.
You could also be given a question about
how lifestyle choices contribute to serious illness.
Moreover, a common task type asks you to
discuss both sides of an argument, such as
whether junk food companies should be held responsible
for rising obesity rates.
Other questions ask you to present your own
opinion on whether free healthcare should be available
to everyone.
It's extremely important that you know specific vocabulary
for this topic.
Learn words like preventable, sedentary lifestyle, and public
health campaign.
Don't simply write people are not healthy.
Instead, use more precise language like this.
A growing proportion of the population leads an
increasingly sedentary lifestyle.
To showcase your grammar range, use cause and
effect structures to explain the relationship between behaviour
and health outcomes.
For example, because many people rely heavily on
processed food for convenience, diet-related conditions such
as type 2 diabetes have become far more
prevalent.
You can also use conditional sentences to discuss
solutions.
If governments invested more in preventative healthcare rather
than treatment, the long-term burden on public
health systems could be reduced considerably.
Another effective technique is using the passive voice
to present issues in a more academic way.
For instance, billions of pounds are spent annually
treating conditions that could have been avoided through
better education and early intervention.
Make sure you develop your arguments with specific
evidence and logical reasoning.
This allows you to show the examiners that
your English is advanced.
Topic number two is environment.
Environmental questions appear frequently in IELTS writing tasks
too, and they can take many forms.
Questions can focus on whether economic development should
take priority over environmental protection.
Some questions focus on what individuals can do
to reduce their carbon footprint.
Another common question is whether governments are doing
enough to tackle climate change.
Essential vocabulary includes words like carbon emissions, deforestation,
renewable energy, and ecological damage.
Don't simply write the environment is being harmed.
Instead, use more precise alternatives like this.
The long-term consequences of unchecked pollution are
becoming increasingly difficult to ignore.
For grammar, use contrast structures to present a
balanced argument.
For example, while rapid industrialisation has undeniably raised
living standards for many, it has also placed
enormous strain on the planet's natural resources.
Moreover, demonstrate your grammar range with conditional sentences
to discuss future consequences.
If current levels of deforestation continue unchecked, vast
areas of biodiversity could be lost within a
generation.
You can also use hedging language to present
uncertain outcomes.
Hedging means using words or phrases that show
you are not completely certain about something.
Take a look at this example.
It seems increasingly likely that rising global temperatures
will have severe consequences for food production and
coastal communities in the coming decades.
In this sentence, the phrases it seems and
increasingly likely show that you are not completely
certain about the effect of rising global temperatures.
Topic number three is education.
This topic comes up a lot in task
two and it covers a wide range of
debates.
One question type asks whether university education should
be free.
Some questions explore whether schools should place more
emphasis on practical skills than academic study.
Others look at whether technology is changing the
way we learn in positive or negative ways.
Key vocabulary includes critical thinking, academic achievement, vocational
training, and higher education.
Don't just write education is very important.
Instead, use more expressive language like this.
A well-rounded education equips individuals with the
skills and knowledge they need to contribute meaningfully
to society.
To showcase your grammar, use infinitives like this.
Schools are designed not only to transmit knowledge
but also to develop the social and analytical
skills that young people will rely on throughout
their working lives.
You can also use concession structures to acknowledge
opposing views.
Although online learning offers greater flexibility and accessibility,
it cannot fully replicate the collaborative environment that
traditional classrooms provide.
By the way, if you want this channel
to keep going, write yes in the comments,
click like, and subscribe.
We only make videos that you actually want
to watch.
Now let's continue with seven more topics that
are common in IELTS writing task two.
Topic number four is society and social issues.
This is a broad topic that covers many
questions.
For instance, questions can ask you to discuss
issues like income inequality.
Some questions focus on the ageing population.
Others explore the role of community in modern
life.
Essential vocabulary includes social cohesion, marginalised communities, income
disparity, and welfare system.
Don't only use simple sentences like this.
There is a big gap between rich and
poor.
Instead, use more precise language like this.
Wealth inequality continues to widen in many developed
nations, placing significant pressure on social mobility.
To showcase your grammar range, use the passive
voice.
Many low-income families are being left behind
by economic policies that disproportionately benefit the wealthy.

transcripts.title: 001.C1C2.READINGIELTS. LOST AND FOUND

segments.text: LOST AND FOUND Lost Indian boy finds his mother after 25 years – using Google Earth
Saroo Brierley was only five years old when he got lost.
He was working with his older brother as a sweeper on India's trains.
Together, they travelled hundreds of miles up and down the vast network.
It was late at night.
We got off the train, and I was so tired that I just sat down at the train station, and I ended up falling asleep.
That fateful nap would determine the rest of his life.
He thought his brother would come back for him, but when he woke up the brother was nowhere to be seen.
There was a train in front of him.
Perhaps his brother was on board.
He got on it to look for him.
However, Saroo did not meet his brother on the train.
Instead, he fell asleep again and when he woke up 14 hours later, he had a terrible shock.
There were hordes of people, pushing, rushing, speaking in an unfamiliar tongue.
He had arrived in Calcutta (Kolkata), India's third biggest city and notorious for its slums.
He was nearly 1,500 kilometres (930 miles) from his home. Soon, he was sleeping rough.
It was a very scary place to be.
I do not think any mother or father would like to have their five-year- old wandering alone in the slums and train stations of Calcutta.
. I was scared. I didn't know where I was.
The little boy learned to fend for himself.
He became a beggar, one of the many children begging on the streets of the city.
Once he was approached by a man who promised him food and shelter.
But Saroo was suspicious and ran away
In the end, Saroo was taken in by an orphanage and put up for adoption.
He was adopted by the Brierleys, a couple from Tasmania.
I accepted that I was lost and that I could not find my way back home,
, so I thought it was great that I was going to Australia.
Saroo settled down well in his new home.
But, as he got older, the desire to find his birth family became increasingly strong.
The problem was that as an illiterate five-year-old he had not known the name of his hometown.
All he had to go on were his vivid memories.
So, he began using Google Earth to search for where he might have been born.
It was difficult, but eventually Saroo hit on an effective strategy.
I multiplied the time I was on the train, about 14 hours,
with the speed of Indian trains and I came up with a rough distance, about 1,200km.
He drew a circle on a map with its centre in Calcutta, with its radius about the distance he thought he had travelled.
Incredibly, he soon discovered what he was looking for: the town of Khandwa.
When I found it, I zoomed down and bang - the waterfall where I used to play
He journeyed to Khandwa.
He found his way around the town with his childhood memories.
Eventually, he found his home.
But it was not what he had hoped for.
It looked old and shabby, as if nobody had lived there for a long time.
A neighbour said that his family had moved.
Then he struck gold, another neighbour said he knew where his mother lived now.
The man guided Saroo to where three women stood waiting.
He stared at them blankly.
Only the woman in the middle seemed remotely familiar.
The man gestured towards her. This is your mother,' he said.
She had been young, in her 30s, the last time he saw her.
She looked so much older now.
But behind the weathered face, there was something unmistakable, unforgettable, his mother, Fatima.
The last time I saw her she was 34 years old and a pretty lady.
I had forgotten that age would get the better of her.
But then I recognised her and I said, 'Yes, you are my mother.'
She grabbed my hand and took me into her house.
She could not say anything to me.
She had a bit of trouble grasping that her son, after 25 years, had returned.
She had long feared I was dead
Fatima had searched the train stations for her missing son, but she had never ventured as far as Calcutta.
She couldn't imagine he had gone so far.
However, she had never lost hope - a fortune teller had told her that one day she would see her son again.
And what of the brother with whom Saroo had originally gone travelling?
Unfortunately, the news was not good.
A month after I had disappeared, my brother was found in two pieces on a railway track.
We were extremely close.
When I left my mother to return to Tasmania
I was heartbroken knowing that my older brother had passed away
His mother had never known whether foul play was involved or whether the boy had simply slipped and fallen under a train.
Saroo Brierley's lifelong wish had been to see his birth mother again.
He feels incredibly grateful that this wish was granted.
He has kept in touch with his newly found family.
And now Hollywood studios have made a star-studded, award-winning film about his amazing story.

transcripts.title: Can the Internet Replace Schools? | IELTS Writing Task 2 Band 8+ Sample Answer

segments.text: Hello! Welcome back to another opinion essay. This question is
based on a recent real IELTS Writing Task 2 topic that appeared in the test. Today,
I'll break it down and share a high-scoring sample answer to help you prepare.
As usual, step one is to read the prompt to find the topic:
Some people believe that since children now have access to the internet,
they no longer need to attend school and can learn at home using the internet.
Now, the instruction: To what extent do you agree or disagree with this statement? So,
you know that we need to take a clear side—to say if we agree or disagree. You can also say,
"I partially agree" or "I agree to some extent," but take a very clear side.
So, my answer is: I disagree. Why? Two reasons to support that:
Schools provide structure and discipline. Schools build social skills.
Now, you know that we don’t have to cover the other side. However,
in order to get a higher band score for Task Response, you need to mention the
opposite side as well to strengthen your argument. Therefore, I'm going to mention
the opposite side and write a paragraph on it. I’ve got two reasons for that as well:
The internet lets children learn at their own speed.
Learning at home is easier with online classes and tools.
Now it’s time to write. The first thing to write is the
introduction. I’m going to paraphrase the prompt and then answer the question.
The internet has revolutionized education in recent years. Some argue that this
development makes traditional schooling unnecessary, as children can study from home.
Now I need to answer the question:
While the internet provides vast resources and learning opportunities,
I believe it cannot fully replace attending school.
I clearly took a position and said that I disagree with the statement.
If you want, you can briefly answer the question as well to improve the cohesion
and coherence of your writing, but I’m going to do that in my body paragraphs.
Before doing that, let’s focus on the useful vocabulary in this paragraph:
Revolutionize: Changed something completely to make it much better.
Schooling: The education children get in school.
Replace: To take the place of something. Now, here are some useful collocations:
In recent years. Traditional schooling.
Study from home. Vast resources.
Learning opportunities. Fully replace.
Attending school. Make something unnecessary.
Remember, in order to get a higher band score for your vocabulary or lexical resource,
you need to use relevant collocations related to the topic. That’s why I’m highlighting these here.
Now, let’s continue with the first body paragraph. The first reason I mentioned is that schools
provide structure and discipline. I need to turn this into a clear topic sentence:
Firstly, schools offer a structured
learning environment that is difficult to replicate at home.
The first thing the examiner asks when they read the topic sentence is why or how. Now,
I need to provide an explanation:
Attending classes ensures that children follow a set curriculum and progress systematically
through various subjects. Teachers play a critical role in delivering content,
addressing students’ questions, and offering guidance.
Example:
For example, a student struggling with math can receive immediate clarification,
which may not happen in an online setting.
The last sentence I’ll add is called a concluding sentence. It’s not a must,
but it strengthens my paragraph:
Without this structure, children learning at home
might struggle with discipline and lack the motivation to complete their studies.
Useful words:
Structured: Organized and planned in a clear way. Replicate: To make or do something
again in the same way. Ensure: To make sure something happens.
Curriculum: A plan of what students will learn in school.
Systematically: In an organized and planned way. Various: Different kinds of something.
Critical: Very important or necessary. Addressing: Dealing with or solving a problem.
Struggling with: To find something difficult to do.
Immediate clarification: Quick and clear explanation.
Discipline: Following rules to stay focused and behave well.
Collocations:
A structured learning environment. Attending classes.
A set curriculum. Progress systematically.
Various subjects. Play a critical role in something.
Delivering content. Addressing students’ questions.
Offering guidance. Receive immediate clarification.
Online setting. Lack motivation.
Complete their studies. All right, it’s time to
write the second body paragraph. The second point I mentioned
is that schools build social skills. I’ll turn this into a clear topic sentence:
Secondly, schools are extremely vital for the development of social skills.
Why or how? I need to explain and provide examples:
Interacting with peers teaches children how to collaborate,
communicate, and resolve conflicts—skills that are essential for success in adulthood.
Example:
For instance, participating in extracurricular activities, such as drama clubs or sports teams,
allows children to practice teamwork in a real-world setting.
Concluding sentence:
Online learning, while informative, often lacks opportunities for meaningful social interaction,
which can lead to feelings of isolation.
Useful words:
Vital: Very important and necessary. Interact with: Talk or work with someone.
Collaborate: To work together with others to achieve something.
Resolve: To solve a problem or find an answer. Extracurricular activities: Activities outside
of regular classes, like sports or clubs. Real-world setting: A situation
or place that is like everyday life. Informative: Giving useful or helpful information.
Isolation: Being alone or separated from others. Collocations:
Extremely vital. Development of social skills.
Resolve conflicts. Something is essential for something else.
Participating in extracurricular activities. Practice teamwork in a real-world setting.
Online learning. Lack opportunities.
Social interaction. Can lead to feelings of isolation.
The third body paragraph is on the opposite side. I mentioned these two points:
The internet lets children learn at their own speed.
Learning at home is easier with online classes and tools.
I’ll write these together in one paragraph:
Admittedly, the internet is a powerful tool for self-directed learning. It offers flexibility,
allowing students to learn at their own pace.
Second point:
Additionally, homeschooling has become more viable through access to high-quality online courses,
virtual classrooms, and tailored learning platforms. Parents can now design effective
homeschool programs that cater specifically to their children’s learning style and unique needs.
Useful words:
Admittedly: Used to show you agree something is true.
Self-directed: Done by yourself without needing someone else to tell you what to do.
Flexibility: The ability to change or adapt easily to different situations.
Pace: The speed at which something happens or is done.
Homeschooling: Teaching children at home instead of sending them to school.
Viable: Possible and likely to work well. Tailored: Made to fit
specific needs or requirements. Cater: To provide what is needed or wanted.
Unique: Special or one of a kind. Collocations:
A powerful tool. Self-directed learning.
Learn at their own pace. Access to online courses.
Virtual classrooms. Learning platforms.
Effective homeschool programs. Learning style and unique needs.
Finally, let’s write the conclusion. I’ll start
with In conclusion and restate my opinion and main points:
In conclusion, the internet has made homeschooling a more practical and
effective option for some families, but it still cannot fully serve as a complete
alternative to the comprehensive benefits of traditional schooling.
Summary of main points:
Schools provide essential structure, socialization,
and a variety of experiences that are hard to achieve at home.
Recommendation:
Therefore, I believe combining the strengths of both online learning
and formal education is the best way forward for children’s development.
Useful words:
Practical: Useful and effective in real situations.
Serve as: To act as or work as something. Alternative: Something different
that can be used instead. Comprehensive: Covering everything that is needed.
Socialization: Learning how to behave and interact with others in society.
A way forward: A solution or plan for the future. Collocations:
A practical and effective option. Complete alternative to.
Comprehensive benefits of traditional schooling.
Essential structure. A variety of.
Hard to achieve. The strength of formal education.
The best way forward for children’s development. I wrote 320 words for this essay. I know it’s
long—probably the longest I’ve written on this channel—but I wanted to show
that it’s important to develop your answers to get a higher band score for Task Response.
I organized my essay in five paragraphs:
In the introduction, I paraphrased and answered the question.
In my first and second body paragraphs, I supported my opinion.
In the third one, I mentioned the opposite side to develop my arguments.
In my conclusion, I restated my opinion,
summarized the main points, and made a recommendation.
All right! Hope you found this useful. Have a nice day!

transcripts.title: Advanced Vocabulary for IELTS Writing Task 2: 19 Useful Phrases and Collocations

segments.text: This is the paragraph we're going to read and analyze. Before we start, you should
pause the video, read the paragraph, and try to guess the meaning of the highlighted words.
Alright, I assume you've done this. Let's begin.
So, the first sentence: 'Culture and language are inextricably intertwined.' 'Inextricably'
is an adverb used to emphasize that two things always exist together and cannot be separated.
For example, 'His name was inextricably linked with the environmental movement.'
Here we have two collocations: 'inextricably linked with'
and 'environmental movement.' 'Intertwined' is an adjective meaning twisted together or closely
connected. For example, 'Personal and business relationships are closely intertwined in Japan.'
Good collocation here: 'personal and business relationships' and 'closely intertwined.'
Moving on to the second sentence: 'Yet, it proves extremely difficult to define precisely what
culture is.' 'Define' is a verb that means to say what the meaning of something, especially a word,
is. For example, 'Before I answer your question, could you define your terms a little more?'
'Precisely' is an adverb that means exactly. For example, 'The fireworks begin at eight
o'clock precisely.' The second one: 'What do you think the problem is precisely?'
The third sentence: 'However, it is doubtful whether culture as such would be teachable.'
'Doubtful' is an adjective that means unlikely to happen or be successful. For example,
'It is doubtful whether they ever reached the summit before they died.'
The term 'normal' is difficult to define. Two good collocations here:
'doubtful weather' and 'doubtful if reached the summit.' 'Teachable' means something that can be
taught. We hope that pupils come to realize that art is a teachable subject, not one that can be
left to talent alone. 'Pupils come to realize' is a good collocation, and also 'teachable subject.'
The next sentence: 'However, it is undeniable that an awareness of culture
and cultural differences is an essential part of an advanced proficiency in using
a language.' 'Undeniable' is an adjective that means certainly true. For example,
'It is an undeniable fact.' 'Awareness' is a noun that means knowledge and understanding of
a particular activity, subjects, etc. For example, 'The guidelines are an important tool for raising
awareness about current management thinking.' Good collocations here: 'important guidelines
tool for raising awareness about' and 'current management thinking.' 'Essential' is another
adjective that means necessary or needed. It's very useful in Writing Task 2. 'Government support
will be essential if the project is to succeed.' 'For the experiment to be valid, it is essential
to record the data accurately.' Collocations: 'government support' and 'valid experiments.'
'It is essential to record the data' and 'record something accurately.'
'Proficiency' is a noun that means great skill, ability, and experience. For example,
'Candidates must be able to demonstrate a high level of proficiency in both
languages.' Collocations: 'be able to,' 'a high level of,' and 'proficiency in.'
In the next sentence, we have: 'Students at this level also need to be aware of the
fast-changing and hybrid nature of culture in an increasingly globalized world.'
'Fast-changing' is an adjective that means developing or changing very quickly. For example,
'Companies such as these need to adapt constantly in fast-changing markets.' 'Adapt constantly,'
'fast-changing markets,' 'fast-changing world of,' and 'a way of life' are good collocations here.
The next word is 'hybrid,' which is an adjective that means a mixture of different things or
styles. 'Difficulties with normal English are its hybrid vocabulary and the irregularities
of English spelling.' Collocations: 'difficulties with normal English,'
'hybrid vocabulary,' and 'irregularities of.'
The next word is 'increasingly,' which is an adverb that means more and more all
the time. For example, 'Increasingly, there is pressure on the council to reverse its decision.'
'Pressure on,' 'reverse its decision,' 'playing an important role,'
'increasingly important,' and 'region's economy' are good collocations.
The last word on this slide is 'globalized,' which is an adjective that means including or
affecting the whole world. For example, 'The new globalized economy.' 'Nature' is a noun here and
means the type or main characteristic of something or the basic qualities of a thing. For example,
'What was the nature of his inquiry?' Collocations: 'nature of,'
'by nature a dangerous sport,' 'changing nature of,' 'define the exact nature of,'
'explores the nature of,' and 'mother-child relationship.'
And the last sentence: 'The focus in this chapter, then, is not to try to teach culture but rather to
encourage learners to engage with the realities of cultural differences, with how it operates,
and how it intersects with language.' 'Encourage' is a useful verb that means to
make someone more likely to do something or to make something more likely to happen. For example,
'We were encouraged to learn foreign languages at school.' 'Recreation' is a way of enjoying
yourself when you're not working. Collocations: 'encouraged to learn foreign languages,'
'encouraging the development of something,' 'employment and recreation.'
The last word on the slide, 'engage,' means to become involved or have
contact with someone or something. Here, it's used in a formal sense.
'Just stay out of his way as much as possible and don't engage with them.'
And the last words: 'Reality' is a noun, a thing that is actually experienced or seen,
in contrast to what people might imagine. For example, 'As children,
they experience the harsh realities of life.'
Collocations: 'experience the harsh realities,' 'reflects the realities,' 'harsh realities,'
'realities of life,' 'reflects the realities,' 'intersects with,' and 'shape our values.'
Alright, I hope you found it useful. Before you use these collocations in
your speaking or writing, make sure you know how to use them,
and read these examples again and again. Have a nice day!

transcripts.title: How to write a good essay: Paraphrasing the question

segments.text: Hi, there. My name is Emma, and in today's video I'm going to teach you something very
important for if you're taking any type of test that has a writing component. So, if
you are taking the IELTS, the TOEFL, the CELPIP, even just a university test, it can be any
type of test, but if you're asked to write something like an essay or a paragraph, this
video is for you. Okay? So I'm going to teach you a very important skill that will help
improve your marks when it comes to writing on tests. So, let's get started.
So, I have here an essay question. This question is actually... I've seen it on the IELTS.
You know, you have similar types of questions on the TOEFL, sometimes in university. The
question is this: "Education is the single most important factor in the development of
a country. Do you agree or disagree?" Or maybe: "To what extent do you agree or disagree?"
So, this is an example of a question you might be asked.
Now, a problem a lot of students have is in their answer to this question. They see this,
and they think: "Okay, education is the most important factor in the development of a country,
yes, I agree." So then they... Or: "I disagree", and they start writing. And what do they write?
Usually the very first thing students will write is this:
"I agree that education is the single most important factor in the development of a country because..." So, what is the problem
with this? Is there any problem to start off your essay with something like this, or to
start off your answer? There's a big problem. So I want you to take a moment and think:
"What could be the problem with starting your essay off with this sentence?"
Okay, well, if you noticed, you have here the word: "education, education, is, is, the single most important,
most important factor". If you notice, these are the same. They're the exact same, except
for: "I agree that" and "because". The student, here, has used the exact same wording that
is in the question. So, if you do this on the IELTS-and many students do this, same
with on the TOEFL-you actually will lose marks, and same with in university, because you're
not showing your abilities; you're just copying what somebody else has said or what the essay
question is.
So, in this video, I'm going to show you first off... First off, I'm going to tell you: Don't
do this, don't copy. And I'm going to teach you ways in order to improve yourself and
your answer by changing this wording. How can you change your introduction so it's different
than what the question is? Okay? So, let's look at how to make these changes.
Okay, so what we are going to do in order to change the question into a proper answer
that doesn't just copy the question, is we are going to paraphrase. So, the word here
is: "paraphrase". This might be a new word for you. What does it mean to paraphrase something?
Well, when we paraphrase, it means we take a sentence that, you know... We take somebody
else's sentence and we change it into our own words. Okay? So, we change the words of
a sentence, we also change maybe the sentence structure, but we keep all the same meaning.
Okay? So, the meaning from the sentence you copy, it stays the same, same meaning, but
different words and different sentence structure. Okay? So it's in your words, but this other
person's meaning.
So, we are going to paraphrase this example of a question into our own words. So, first
we're going to look at how to do that using vocabulary and synonyms. So, we have here
the same question: "Education is the single most important factor in the development of
a country." How can we put this into new words or our own words that keep the same meaning?
Well, we can use synonyms. So, this might be a new word for you, too. A "synonym". "Synonyms"
are words that have the same meaning, but are different words. So, for example: "big"
and "large", they have the same meaning, just like: "huge", "enormous", these are synonyms
of each other; same meaning, but they're different words. So, you need to use different synonyms
so you don't just copy these words. You use synonyms to have words that have the same
meaning, but are different words.
So, let's look at an example. Our first word, here, is: "education".
What's another word we can use instead of...? Instead of "education"? Well, there's different words we can use.
Maybe one could be: "schooling". Okay? So, we could change this word to "schooling".
"Schooling is the single most important factor in the development of a country." What's another
word we can change? Well, maybe "most important". Instead of using the word "most important",
maybe we could use: "most significant" or "most essential". Okay? So: "essential", "significant".
There are many words you can use. But the point here is: Find a word that has the same
meaning, but is a different word.
Okay, here's another word: "factor". Can you think of another word for "factor"? Well,
sometimes "factor", it can be an "aspect" or an "element". Okay? You can even say sometimes:
"a significant role". Okay? Or: "a part". So, there's different words we can use that
have similar meanings. In terms of the word "development", we can change the word "development"
to "advancement", "progression", "evolution". Okay? And in terms of the word "country",
another word for "country" is "nation". Okay?
So, these are all synonyms, and this is what you want to do. When you look at the question,
think about some new words you can use that have the same meaning. This is also important
throughout your essay, because one problem a lot of students have is they keep using
the same word again, and again, and again in every sentence. This does not help you
with your marks. It's better to use different words that have the same meaning. At the same
time, you have to be careful, here, because some students, they find a new word, they
think it's a great word, but there is a little bit of a difference in meaning. So, you need
to be really comfortable with the word you choose, and you need to know what it actually
means so it doesn't sound strange.
Okay, so if we wanted to change this now, instead of saying:
"Education is the single most important factor in the development of a country",
our paraphrase... Our first step
in our paraphrase could be changing these words to:
"Schooling is the single most significant element in the advancement of a nation."
Okay? So that's just one example. So, now, let's
look at another thing you can do in order to paraphrase the question on a test or exam.
Okay, another way we can paraphrase is by changing the structure of the sentence. So,
for example, you might have a verb and, you know, which is an action, and you might change
that into its noun form. So, for example, if your verb is "developing", you might change
that into "development". You might change, similarly, a noun into a verb. Okay? So, for
example, we just said "development" is the noun, it can turn into: "develop" or "developing".
You can also change things into adjectives. So, if, for example, you're talking about
"technology", which is a noun, you can change this into the adjective form which is "technological".
So, changing the form of the word can help you with paraphrasing.
Also changing placement of the words can help you out. So, for example, in our original
sentence or the question was:
"Education is the single most important factor in the development of a country."
So, I've now changed some of the wording, as well as the order. Okay? So,
here: "Education" is at the beginning. In my sentence:
"The most essential element of a nation's development is education."
I've changed the order of the sentence, so now
"education" is at the end, instead of at the beginning. I've also started out with:
"The most essential", as my beginning; whereas here, it was in the middle. You'll also notice
we have, here: "in the development of a country", I've changed this to: "nation's development".
I could also change this to: "country's development", instead of "the development of a country".

help you in terms of paraphrasing. Now let's learn one other way in which we can improve
our marks by paraphrasing.

is a concession? Well, I want you to look at what the question actually says and my
new answer to it. The question, again, same question as before:
"Education is the single most important factor in the development of a country."
My answer... Okay, so I've changed some words and I've also changed the structure a bit, but there's one other thing I've added.
"Although many would argue that the economy is the most important factor in nation-building,
I think education has a far greater impact." So, what I've done here is I've added a concession.
A concession is where you say what the opposite opinion is, and then you say what your opinion
is. So, you're giving two opinions; you're giving your opinion and also what other people
might think. This is a great thing to do, especially in essays, and this is something
you can do at the beginning of your answer.
So, we use here the key word: "Although". Okay? And you'll notice that this has two
clauses. I don't want to get too technical with grammar on you today, but what I mean
is: If you see, we have the red part: "Although many would argue that the economy is the most
important factor in nation-building," and then we have a second part. So, we have two
parts to this sentence. "...I think education has a far greater impact." So, the first part
of the sentence is in red and it's the "Although" part, and the second part of the sentence
is in purple-okay?-and that's: "I think education has a far greater impact."
And they're separated by a comma. So, a concession has two parts to it. You say what the other people think
first, in this case: "Although many", "many" meaning people, we could also say: "many people".
"Although many would argue that the economy is the most important factor in nation-building,"
okay? So, this is what some people would say. Now I'm going to say what I think.
"I think education has a far greater impact."
So, why is this a good idea? Well, one reason is because when you write a concession, when
you're showing what the opposite opinion of yours is, you're showing that you've thought
about the issue. Okay? You're looking at both sides. You're not just looking at your opinion.
You're looking at both sides, and then you're making a judgment. So this shows that you're
thinking about the question, and you're really giving it some thought. And by representing
both sides, you're really showing critical thinking. So this is a very good idea to do.
Okay, so the three things we've talked about today in terms of paraphrasing the question
is: Changing the words using synonyms, we've talked about changing the sentence structure,
and we've also talked about adding the other perspective using concessions. Okay? So, these
are three things you can do in order to change the question so you're not just copying what
is on your test paper. Again, great thing to do if you're writing a high school essay,
university essay, TOEFL, IELTS, CELPIP, all of these things - this skill will really come
in handy for you.
Now, you might be wondering: "Okay, this is great, but I don't know any synonyms. Or I...
You know, I don't know much about this." Well, what you can do is you can come check out
our website at www.engvid.com. There, you can find a lot of other resources, including
improving your vocabulary, we even have a video on how to make concessions, you know,
we have videos on sentence structure, too. So, there's a lot of videos you can check

see, you know: "Am I doing this right? Is this...? You know, is this the right way to
do this?" and get more practice.
So, I hope you've enjoyed this video.
And until next time, take care.
